text: She had clinically staged 2B T3N0M0 CWRT1 GE junction adenocarcinoma and underwent preoperative chemoradiation per cross-trial regimen.
She had a good response on restaging PET scan and was deemed to have good baseline functional status.
Here you can see the varus needle insufflation of the abdomen as well as the patient's prior surgical scars.
No injury is noted on entry into the abdomen and there is no evidence of metastatic disease.
We place a 12mm camera port, a 15mm working port, and three 5mm ports, along with one small stab incision for the liver retractor.
This allows both the primary and assisting surgeons to each use two instruments.
The procedure is begun with division of the gastrohepatic ligament using a 5mm blunt ligature device.
The lesser curve of the stomach is dissected to the level of the hiatus, and here you can see the GE junction is partially dissected.
A penrose drain is then placed for later retrieval around the esophagus.
The anterior leaflet of the omentum is then incised several centimeters lateral to the gastroepiploic artery to enter the lesser sac.
Lysis of adhesions is also performed at this time due to the patient's prior abdominal surgeries.
The dissection is then continued along the greater curvature of the stomach up to the hiatus, including division of the short gastric arteries.
Care is taken to stay several centimeters lateral to the gastroepiploic artery.
The pedicle is never grasped, nor is it used for retraction.
Direct grasping of the conduit itself is also avoided.
Lysis of adhesions is then continued anteriorly and further dissection is performed to carefully separate the transverse colon from the antrum, pylorus, and first portion of the duodenum.
Great care is taken when dissecting the mesocolon from the gastroepiploic pedicle.
The remainder of the posterior attachments of the stomach are then divided.
The stomach is now nearly fully mobilized as you can see here.
The penebrous drain is then secured around the GE junction.
This aids in manipulation of the stomach and dissection of the left gastric artery.
The dissection is carried out from both the posterior and anterior sides of the stomach.
The celiac plexus and lymph nodes are isolated and removed prior to firing a purple load stapler, which then divides the left gastric artery pedicle.
From here, we proceed with dissection of the hiatus.
Circumferential dissection around the esophagus is carried out into the mediastinum.
At this point, the stomach is well mobilized and the nasogastric tube is drawn back.
Tubularization has begun along the lesser curve of the stomach using multiple loads of a thick tissue stapler.
Complete tubularization of the stomach will occur later during the thoracic phase of the operation.
Of note, feeding jejunostomy tube placement and pyloroplasty are not regularly performed.
The abdominal portion of the operation comes to a close as the Penrose drain is placed within the newly dissected thoracic cavity and the port sites are closed.
The patient is then repositioned and the right chest is prepped and draped.
Four one-centimeter thoracic ports are placed along the mid, anterior, and posterior axillary lines, along with a three-centimeter utility port in the posterior eighth intercostal space.
No metastatic disease is noted within the chest cavity.
The pleura is dissected toward the azagus vein with care taken to preserve the pleural edge for later closure of the mediastinal envelope.
The azagus vein is then divided with a vascular stapler load.
Here the posterior mediastinal pleural edge is prepared to aid in later closure.
The penderose is clearly visualized from the prior mediastinal dissection and is used to manipulate the esophagus as it's circumferentially mobilized within the chest cavity.
Great care is taken not to injure the thoracic duct.
And the balloon cuff is let down during dissection near the left main bronchus in order to avoid thermal injury.
Once fully mobilized, the esophagus is then incised with longitudinal extension.
And a 28 EEA stapler anvil is placed in the proximal portion of the esophagus.
Division of the esophagus is completed and the anvil is secured with two ochromic endeloop snare ligatures.
The excess tissue is removed so as not to interfere with the anastomosis.
The distal esophagus and gastric conduit are then retrieved from the abdomen intact.
A gastrotomy is performed in the lesser curvature of the stomach that had already been partially stapled off.
The gastrotomy is longitudinally extended and the EEA stapler is then placed through it, aiming toward the body and fundus of the stomach.
The stapler spike is deployed completely through the gastric tissue and then secured to the anvil.
Care is taken to ensure that there is no lung or omentum within the anastomosis.
Stapler is then fired and upon removal, the donuts are inspected and found to be intact.
The proximal donut is noted to be the proximal margin.
Several loads of the thick tissue stapler are used to complete the tubularization of the stomach.
While ICG immunofluorescence was previously used for a period of time to inspect the anastomosis, this is no longer our routine practice.
The final specimen is removed through a wound protector in the posterior utility port, and an azigastric tube is placed under laparoscopic guidance.
The chest cavity is then irrigated.
The lower mediastinal envelope is then closed up to the level of the azagus vein.
Omentum is placed along the EEA anastomosis and draped along the tubularized conduit.
Closing the mediastinal envelope helps secure the conduit in a manner that avoids pooling of food particulate.
This helps facilitate the ability to achieve early nasogastric tube removal and PO tolerance.
Intercostal nerve blockade is performed with liposomal bupivacaine to aid with prolonged postoperative pain control.
A 28 French chest tube and 24 French Blake drain are placed and the lungs are visualized as they re-expand.
This patient tolerated the procedure well and went to the recovery room and then floor.
